item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, that was so good. I mean, it's still good now, in the present, and will be good in the future, and also will have been good, but I meant that I really enjoyed reading it. I am still experiencing enjoyment.

Sorry I said "dude" but I couldn't help it after reading that rollercoaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why was the title of this changed to Predatory Bus-iness Practices? Was "Stairway to Heaven" a copyright violation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was missing the

« SCP-2987 | SCP-2968 | SCP-2969 »

I added it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The typos in the second log are deliberate, and are supposed to reflect Wei's growing panic. Someone edited this to fix those, and I have reverted the changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a recent SMBC comic that's awfully similar to this. Also press the red button at the bottom for the bonus panel, for even more similarity!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The previous version of the article was deleted for some reason. You can see the old text here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are several other pages which apparently link to an SCP of the same number as this, but refer to a vastly different item, some kind of biomechanical insect colony? Not sure what's going on here although the HH pills SCP is a decent effort in itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last log for sure sealed the deal for me, I got a sort of Heaven's Gate meets tv infomercial scammers vibe from this, and I like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His mind isn't being controlled, and the things responsible don't really care about how he thinks except instrumentally — the human mind needs to be in a certain state so that it can be slurped up.

I was actually aiming somewhat towards the more altered side of the spectrum. Rudi can't see anymore that being told not to be afraid isn't what Wei needs, and can't really empathize anymore with Wei's distress. That's the reason the last line is there — Wei is losing his friend in more ways than one.

Certainly though, not control or alteration of the "Sam is gone. What remains shall feast on your souls!" type. That is kind of annoying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I meant that I’m glad they weren’t having their mind completely(?) controlled after their death, spouting eerily vague lines about their own doom or some higher entity. I’ve been coming across that in a lot of new articles— but Rudi feels far more real, even while having their mind possibly swayed by whatever situation they’re in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What do you mean by that about Rudi's personality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, I really like how the interview logs deliver an increasing amount of unease as they continue, all without Rudi's personality turning into a horror trope. This is a great example of a somewhat vanilla anomaly concept hosting a solid, short narrative. Happily upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The typos in the second interview log are indeed intentional. Well, sort of. "Taek" was a legit typo I made, but I decided to leave it rather than fix it because I figured Wei was a bit too frantic right then to be watching what he was typing very closely.

I've sat on this one for a while, and I couldn't quite get it to work. I had the premise — one-a-day afterlife pills — and the conclusion — speed of light delay — but I couldn't quite solder them together. A week and a half ago, I got the idea of having the pills not be placebos as I had had them, but rather alter people's mental states into one that's receptive to being slurped up. That gave me character hooks and a logical reason for why the pills are necessary, letting me bridge the gap.

The initial draft of this concept had a really dry description and didn't have room for foreshadowing or hinting at some stuff in the backstory, so making the section about a preliminary report let me make the description just a description and introduce some things like the idea that the pills cause enlightenment in a less formal format. Though this is darker than most of my SCPs, it also let me squeeze in a bit of humor. I'm pleased with how this turned out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohh, that would make sense. I don't think I quite figured out how they were communicating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe those typos are intentional, kind of as a signal of how quickly they're typing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Second interview has quite a few typos, mostly sentences that either don't have periods or weren't finished.

But this is good! dado's got competition!

Dr. Rudi: Wei, I think we’re rocket fuel.

And holy crap, that's a solid twist right at the end! Very good work. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. That last log is a sucker punch.

I really liked reading this in the review phase, and like it now. I think you do a great job building up the relationship of the two researchers over the course of their discussions, and create an environment for the reader that is ripe for the final twist.

Very effective.

+1.
